attributes.stem:
A 66-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of sharp back pain which has been getting worse over the last few hours. He has numbness and tingling in his legs and difficulty walking. He has not been able to pass urine since his symptoms started. His history includes hypertension and diabetes, for which he takes perindopril and metformin.

On examination his vital signs are within normal limits. He has decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch below the level of T8 while the vibration and proprioception are both intact. His motor strength on MRC 1/5 in his lower limbs. Both knee and ankle jerks are absent with equivocal plantar response.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This presentation with acute onset of back pain, along with the symptoms of numbness, tingling, and motor weakness in the lower limbs, indicate a potential spinal cord lesion. The decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch below the T8 level suggests spinothalamic tract involvement. The presence of motor weakness and equivocal plantar response indicates a corticospinal tract. These findings suggests anterior spinal artery occlusion.

Anterior spinal artery occlusion causes anterior cord syndrome (damage to the ventral cord) which manifests as pyramidal motor weakness (corticospinal tract) and, often, bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation below the level of the lesion (spinothalamic tract). Proprioception and vibration are preserved as the dorsal columns run posteriorly in the cord.

Brown-Séquard syndrome refers to ipsilateral upper motor neuron motor weakness, proprioceptive deficits and impaired appreciation of vibration, with contralateral pain and temperature loss. It is caused by a lesion that involves the lateral half of the cord, affecting the corticospinal, spinothalamic and sometimes, proprioceptive tracts on that side.

Complete cord transection manifests as bilateral upper motor neuron (UMN) pattern weakness and bilateral loss of all types of sensation below level of the lesion. It may be caused by trauma, demyelination, or haemorrhage.

Posterior column syndrome occurs when pathology of one or both dorsal columns of the spinal cord is present. Patients may present with sensory ataxia and have proprioceptive deficits on examination as well as impaired appreciation of vibration. The prototypic cause is vitamin B12 deficiency, leading to subacute combined degeneration of the cord in which the lateral columns are also involved concurrently.

Syringomyelia classically presents as a capelike area of sensory loss to pain and temperature over the upper trunk and arms at the level of the lesion (due to affection of the decussating spinothalamic tract fibers). A characteristic pattern of distal greater than proximal weakness in the upper limbs predominantly the hands (due to damage to the anterior horn cells at that level) ensues. Syringomyelia can occur ion the thoracic cord and therefore spare the upper limbs.
learningPoints: Anterior spinal artery occlusion can lead to a sudden onset of back pain, motor weakness, and superficial sensory deficits below the level of the spinal cord lesion with preservation of deep sensation.
It is important to recognise the specific pattern of symptoms associated with this condition to facilitate early diagnosis and appropriate management.
references: Cho TA. Spinal cord functional anatomy. CONTINUUM Lifelong Learning in Neurology . 2015 Feb 13 ;21:13–35.
